The
house hippo

is the subject of a Canadian television public service announcement (PSA) produced by Concerned Children's Advertisers in 1999. The fake infomercial about the fictional critters was created in order to raise awareness about the importance of media literacy. It was produced in the style of
Hinterland Who's Who
, the Canadian educational series that ran in the 1960s and 1970s. Two decades later, Ottawa-based not-for-profit MediaSmarts brought back the house hippo in a
new commerical
as part of a media literacy campaign called
Break the Fake
. Real or not, the house hippo will always be a nostalgic piece of Canadiana.

A skein is a group of geese flying together in a "V" shaped formation. In the sky, they are called skein, but when a flock of geese is on the ground, they form a gaggle. Did you know that a skein of migrating Canada geese can fly up to 2,400 km in 24 hours? Canada geese travel north between April and early June, so look up in the sky to spot a skein and listen for their distinctive honks!

The
Log Driver's Waltz

is a Canadian folk song written by Wade Hemsworth in the 1950s that was popularized by a National Film Board of Canada video in 1979 as part of a Canada Vignettes series. The soundtrack for the animated short was performed by Canadian folk music group The Mountain City Four. According to Hemsworth's great nephew (also named Wade Hemsworth): "The Log Driver’s Waltz is a song about a time in Canadian history when logging and moving logs to the pulp and paper mill was done in a more traditional fashion. The action of burling on logs, or standing on them, as they turned over and staying upright was a unique skill." Over forty years later, The Log Driver's Waltz remains one of the NFB's most-requested films.

Canadian engineer John D. Millar devised what now seems a straightforward manner of helping drivers to share the road - painting lines to divide lanes and to separate traffic moving in opposite directions. Millar was working for the Ontario Department of Transport when he came up with the idea in 1930. A stretch of highway between Quebec and Ontario was the first to receive the treatment. Millar’s idea soon received wide acceptance — within a few years, white painted lines were standard not only on highways in Ontario but all across North America, and accident rates dropped significantly. Yellow, dashed, and double lines followed as ways of providing additional information to drivers.

The Canadian-built
Canadair Sabre

was a single-engine jet fighter flown by the Golden Hawks - the Royal Canadian Air Force (RCAF) aerobatic flying team that preceded the Snowbirds. The Sabre had a distinctive gold livery with a red and white hawk painted on each side of the fuselage that captivated airshow crowds across Canada.

The Montreal melon, also known as the Montreal market muskmelon or the Montreal nutmeg melon, is a variety of melon recently rediscovered and cultivated in Montreal, Quebec. It was originally widely grown between the St. Lawrence River and Mount Royal, on the Montreal Plain. In its prime from the late 19th century until World War II, it was one of the most popular varieties of melon on the east coast of North America.

International Brotherhood of Magicians

National Nursing Week

! Annual celebrations take place in Canada from the Monday to the Sunday of the same week as Florence Nightingale’s birthday on May 12. Today, Canada is one of the few countries in the world requiring a bachelor’s degree for entry to practice as a registered nurse (RN). In 2021, there were 312,382 RNs licensed to practise in Canada. Named as one of the best Gordon Lightfoot songs of all time, "The Wreck of the Edmund Fitzgerald" tells the real-life story of the Edmund Fitzgerald, an ore carrier that sank in Lake Superior in 1975 and took 29 nine members of the crew with it. Beautifully arranged, and blessed with some truly stupendous lyrics, it remains one of his most poignant and popular songs. Released just one year after the disaster, it sailed to No. 1 in Canada and No. 2 on the US Billboard Hot 100.

He is credited with helping to define the folk-pop sound of the 1960s and 1970s. He has been referred to as Canada's greatest songwriter and was known internationally as a folk-rock legend. #RIPGordonLightfoot

Beaver pelts were an integral part of the early Canadian fur trade economy. The fur trade began in the 1600s in what is now Canada. It continued for more than 250 years. Beaver pelts were among the most sought-after furs due to the popularity of beaver top hats in Europe at the time. While the hunting of beavers was only a small part of life for Indigenous peoples before Europeans came, it soon became the predominant activity in some territories.

The Dionne quintuplets are the first quintuplets known to have survived their infancy. The identical girls were born on May 28, 1934 just outside Callander, Ontario, near the village of Corbeil. The birth and survival of five identical, premature and undersized infants – Yvonne, Annette, Cécile, Émilie, and Marie – was an unprecedented event that attracted international attention. All five survived to adulthood. In 2018, the birth of the quintuplets was named a National Historic Event. Happy 89th Birthday to the Dionne quints!

Canada's wetlands are diverse. They take the form of marshes, bogs, fens, swamps and open water. They appear on the prairies, in the boreal forest, along coastlines and even in the tundra. Marshes are treeless wetlands with an abundance of herbaceous plants, including grasses, sedges, reeds and cattails. They are highly productive ecosystems teeming with life. The marsh at Point Pelee National Park in southern Ontario supports 19 species at risk and is a critical stop-over for migratory birds, which rely on the chance to forage for food in the marsh after a long flight.

Located in Kaslo, British Columbia, the
S.S. Moyie stern wheeler

is the oldest intact passenger steam vessel of its type in the world. The machinery and the steel frames of its hull were built by the Bertram Engine Works of Toronto, shipped west by rail and assembled in Vancouver. Launched at Nelson on 22 October 1898 by the Canadian Pacific Railway, the S.S. Moyie carried passengers and freight on Kootenay Lake for 59 years. It was retired from service in 1957. Rescued and preserved by the people of Kaslo, the "sweetheart of the lake" recalls the role of sternwheelers in the history of western Canada. 2023 marks the 125th birthday celebration of the S.S. Moyie, which was designated a national historic site of Canada in 1958 and reaffirmed in 1983.

An umiak ("open skin boat" in Inuktitut) is an open boat, with a wooden frame and a covering of bearded seal or walrus hide. The umiak was made of driftwood or whale bone, with skins stretched over the frame. Although comparatively light, it could transport up to thirty people and several tonnes of goods. The umiak dates to early Inuit (Thule) times in the central Arctic and appeared in Greenland, Baffin Island, Labrador, the Mackenzie Delta, Alaska and eastern Siberia. Until recent times, the umiak was the primary method of summer transport for coastal Inuit, used for moving family and possessions to seasonal hunting areas and for whaling expeditions.

In 1909, one of the first transatlantic telegraph cables made landfall in Halifax, Nova Scotia, creating a physical communications link between North America and Europe. Another cable linked St. John's to New York, and previously cables ran between Ireland and Heart's Content, Newfoundland in the 1850s and 1860s. Within 20 years, seven more cables connected Europe and North America via Signal Hill. Halifax was the Atlantic seaboard's primary cable ship port and service call depot for the transatlantic cable. Large spools of cable were stored in the bottom of Cable Wharf. As one of the last original structures on the harbour, Cable Wharf is a rare gem among the constantly changing Halifax Waterfront.

One of the first regularly scheduled hovercraft ferries in the world operated out of Vancouver - but it didn't last long. When early fur traders found the creek on their westward treks in the 1800s, they named it "Rivière Au Courant." For quite some time, Swift Current was the freight terminus for Western Canada. As the primary service centre for most of Southwestern Saskatchewan, its name is also frequently contracted to "Swift" or "Swifty", but has no ties to Taylor Swift's fan base of Swifties :)

Happy Labour Day! Labour Day has been marked as a statutory public holiday in Canada on the first Monday in September since 1894. The Printers' Strike of 1872, part of the Nine Hour Movement, was a key moment in Canadian labour history and marked the start of a shorter workday.

Richard Pochinko (1946–1989) was a notable Canadian clown trainer who developed a new style of mask/clown performance training, known as the "Pochinko Method" or the Canadian Clowning technique. Originally from Selkirk, Manitoba, he was based primarily in Toronto, Ontario and founded the city's
Theatre Resource Centre

. Pochinko's technique for which he is known combined both European and First Nations traditions of masked and clown performance.

Canada's first newspaper, the Halifax Gazette, came off the press on March 23, 1752, in a print shop in downtown Halifax. It was printed on a single sheet of paper with two columns of text on each side. The paper was published under various names until 1867 when it became the Nova Scotia Royal Gazette, the official government publication.

Today marks the start of the 2023-24 National Hockey League Season! The goaltender mask is now a mandatory piece of equipment, but that wasn't always the case. The first goaltender to wear a mask in any level of organized ice hockey was Elizabeth Graham of Queen’s University in 1927 who wore a fencing mask. The first NHL goalie to wear a mask full-time was Jacques Plante of the Montreal Canadiens. During the 1959 season, Plante wore a face-hugging fibreglass mask created by Bill Burchmore, a children’s hockey coach and Canadiens fan who worked at Fiberglas Canada in Montreal. Goalie masks have certainly come a long way since then!

The Quiet Revolution ("Révolution tranquille" in French) refers to a significant period of socio-political and socio-cultural transformation in French Canada, particularly in Quebec, following the election of 1960. This vivid yet paradoxical description of the period was first used by an anonymous writer in The Globe and Mail. The Quiet Revolution is a major reference point used by successive Québec governments in power since the Liberal defeat in 1966.

The
Frank Slide

was a massive rockslide that buried part of the mining town of Frank in the District of Alberta of the North-West Territories (now the province of Alberta) on April 29, 1903. The site of the disaster, which remains nearly unchanged since the slide, is now a popular tourist destination. It has been designated a provincial historic site of Alberta and is home to an interpretive centre that receives over 100,000 visitors annually.

Baked beans are one of Canada’s truly national dishes — beginning with Indigenous iterations all the way up to its place on pulse trends of today’s Canada’s food guide. And while many ingredients are incredibly regional, the versatility and history of the humble bean spans from coast to coast. Our favourite style is fèves au lard, a traditional Québécois dish of beans mixed with pieces of bacon and either molasses or maple syrup that is then slow cooked in the oven. Delish!

The ophthalmoscope, a small hand-held telescope for looking through the pupil of the eye, allows doctors to see inside the eye and to examine the retina and optic nerve, which are extensions of the brain. In 1864, Toronto doctors pioneered retinal photography by connecting an ophthalmoscope to a camera to photograph the retina of a cat, a first step in photographing the inside of the living human eye and a technique that today is essential all over the world in evaluating and treating patients with eye diseases.

." This treeless zone covers everything from narrow isolated islands to steep hillsides. Spanning national forests and towering mountains, the vast majority of the Slash is so remote that it will never receive any visitors (aside from a handful of bears).

In response to rising blindness rates caused by the Halifax explosion and veterans returning home from World War I, the
Canadian National Institute for the Blind

(CNIB) was founded and incorporated by a group of seven Canadian men - several veterans and blind men among them. Since the founding in 1918, the CNIB Foundation has been creating programs, providing services and advocating to change the lives of Canadians who are blind or partially sighted. CNIB is a non-profit organization driven to change what it is to be blind today.

Freedom of religion in Canada is a constitutionally protected right, allowing individuals to assemble and worship without limitation or interference.

The local convenience store has been the backbone of Canadian communities for generations. In fact, our convenience store roots can be traced back to Quebec in the 1600s when Samuel de Champlain founded the first store outside of Quebec City. The more than 22,500 stores across Canada act as gathering places in many communities. Those community hubs generate more than $54 billion in sales, employ more than 200,000 Canadians and sell everything from fuel, EV charging, to lottery, energy drinks, bottled water, salty snacks and in Quebec and Newfoundland and Labrador, beer.

It is the most significant rivalry in Canadian university football and is a long-running tradition which has ignited school spirit since 1955. Its name is derived from Pedro the Panda, the trophy presented to the winner each year. The city of
Yellowknife

is the capital of the Northwest Territories and the territory's only city. The city gets its name from the copper tools historically made and traded by the local Dene First Nation. The city and Yellowknife Bay were named after the Yellowknives, a Dene band who lived on the islands of Great Slave's East Arm and travelled as far north as the Arctic coast to obtain copper for knives and other implements. They, in turn, acquired their name from the copper-bladed knives they carried.

Spotted Lake (Lake Kliluk) in British Columbia’s Similkameen Valley is made up of hundreds of separate pools that contain some of the world’s highest concentrations of Epsom salts, calcium, sodium sulphates and magnesium sulphates. When the warm summer months roll in, much of the water evaporates, leaving behind concentrations of the minerals that form spots across the lake. The spots can change in size and colour from blue to green to yellow, as minerals within each spot alter with further evaporation. According to Destination Osoyoos, Spotted Lake is a sacred place of healing for the people of the Syilx Okanagan Nation.

Today is International Inuit Day! It is a holiday that was created to celebrate Inuit and amplify their voices. Inuit throat singing, or katajjaq, is a distinct vocal style uniquely found among the Inuit. It is a form of musical performance, traditionally consisting of two women who sing duets in a close face-to-face formation with no instrumental accompaniment. Their technique relies on short, sharp, rhythmic inhalations and exhalations of breath. The Inuit recognize katajjaniq as part of their cultural heritage.

The mission of
No Stone Left Alone

is to honour the sacrifice and service of Canada’s military by educating students and placing poppies on the headstones of veterans every November. No Stone Left Alone was officially launched in 2011 to help ensure an enduring national respect and gratitude for the sacrifice of the Canadian men and women who have lost their lives in the service of peace, at home and abroad. It has become the mission of founder Maureen Bianchini Purvis to see that one day all of the soldiers' headstones would have a poppy placed in their honour, with truly No Stone Left Alone.

The
Last Steps Memorial Arch

is located on a Halifax pier behind the Maritime Museum of the Atlantic. The Halifax waterfront is where thousands of Canadian men and women took their last steps as they embarked on ships to Europe during the First World War. There were around 350,000 Canadian men and women who boarded ships in Halifax to be taken overseas. About 60,000 never returned. The wooden arch bears the words "The Last Steps," immortalizing the place where many of those soldiers last stood on Canadian soil.

Today is
World Diabetes Day

! Celebrated annually on November 14th, the day marks the birthday of Canadian medical scientist Frederick Banting who, along with Charles Best, first conceived the idea which led to the discovery of insulin in 1922. Insulin is a peptide hormone produced by beta cells of the pancreatic islets. It regulates the metabolism of carbohydrates, fats and protein by promoting the absorption of glucose from the blood into liver, fat and skeletal muscle cells.

Happy National Philanthropy Day! Every year on November 15, this day pays tribute to the extraordinary contribution that those involved in the philanthropic process have made to our communities. In 2012, Canada became the first - and remains the only - country in the world to recognize this day. Philanthropy is the spirit of giving without expectation of reward. Canadians continue to be inspired by the dedication of volunteers who devote themselves to improving the lives of others. Today is Louis Riel Day - a day to reflect on
Louis Riel

, and his important role for the advocacy of the Métis Nation and his help in building the Canada we know today. Louis Riel was a Canadian politician, a founder of the province of Manitoba, and a political leader of the Métis people. He led two resistance movements -  the Red River Rebellion of 1869-70 and the North-West Rebellion of 1885 - against the Government of Canada and its first prime minister John A. Macdonald. Riel's historical reputation has long been polarized between portrayals as a dangerous religious fanatic and rebel opposed to the Canadian nation, and, by contrast, as a charismatic leader intent on defending his Métis people. November 16 is the anniversary of Riel's execution in 1885. In Manitoba, Louis Riel Day takes place on the third Monday of February and commemorates his life.

Trans Canada Trail

is Canada’s national trail and is the longest network of multi-use recreational trails in the world!

Thomas Leopold "Carbide" Willson (March 14, 1860 – December 20, 1915) was a Canadian inventor. He was born on a farm near Princeton, Canada West, in 1860 and went to school in Hamilton, Ontario. By the age of 21, he had designed and patented the first electric arc lamps used in Hamilton. Willson's accidental discovery of calcium carbide in 1892 led to widespread use of carbide lamps in early cars and motorcycles and as headlamps for miners.

110th CFL Grey Cup
! When you hear the term blitz you probably think of football, but The Canadian Wildlife Federation and the Nature Conservancy of Canada use
Bioblitzes

to help determine the health of an ecosystem. A Bioblitz is a participatory search for species within a limited area and a limited timeframe that takes a "snapshot" of as many living things as possible. Biodiversity - literally the variety of life - is an indicator that an area is ecologically stable and healthy. Through use of the
iNaturalist Canada app

, the species records are compiled into a single data set to help researchers, scientists and policy-makers to help them make informed decisions about wildlife management.

Chasm Provincial Park

is a provincial park in British Columbia, Canada, located near the town of Clinton. The park was originally created in 1940 to preserve and promote a feature known as the Painted Chasm, or simply The Chasm, a gorge created from melting glacial waters eroding a lava plateau over a 10 million year span called the Chilcotin Group. The Chasm is approximately 600 metres wide and 8 kilometres long, and lies adjacent to the route of the Cariboo Road.

December 10th is National Lager Day! Lagers are some of the most popular beer styles in the world and Canada is no exception. Pale lagers are the most popular beer in Canada. The term "lager" comes from the German word for "storage", as the beer was stored before drinking, traditionally in the same cool caves in which it was fermented. A stook, also referred to as a shock or stack, is an arrangement of sheaves of cut grain-stalks placed so as to keep the grain-heads off the ground while still in the field and before collection for threshing. Stooked grain sheaves are typically wheat, barley and oats. In Canada, a stook may also refer to a field stack of small, rectangular bales of hay or straw. These bales may be stacked and deposited by a "stooking machine" or "stooker" that is dragged, sled-like, behind the baler.

For the past several weeks, the most popular songs around have been Christmas carols. Many of our favourites have European roots, but one that is still sung can claim to be the first Canadian Christmas carol. The "Huron Carol" (or "Twas in the Moon of Wintertime") was written by Jean de Brébeuf, a Jesuit missionary at Sainte-Marie among the Hurons in Canada. Brebeuf's hymn, written in both Huron and French, is now over 380 years old. The song remains a common Christmas hymn in Canadian churches, and it has become a cherished part of Canadian Christmas.

Merry Christmas from Canuckle! In 1606, Samuel de Champlain created the Order of Good Cheer in Port-Royal, Nova Scotia. The Order of Good Cheer was a winter supper club, created to pass the winter months joyously and to combat French settlers from dying of scurvy. Each member of the Order of Good Cheer had to take his turn hunting, fishing, and trading with the Mi’kmaq to create a lavish meal. The night would begin with a ceremonial procession to the table with members carrying the dishes to the feast. The feast leader would lead the procession and he would have the honour of wearing the chain of the Order and a large napkin over his shoulder. To create a lively and fun atmosphere, the dinners would be filled with lots of story-telling, singing and music. Cheers! We hope your day is full of Christmas cheer!

GGC implemented the name change after hearing from current and former members that the previous name caused harm and was a barrier to belonging for racialized girls and women. With the new Embers name, the hope is that even more girls will see Guiding as a space where they can just be themselves.

The
Cuban Lunch

bar was a chocolate bar originally produced in Winnipeg by the Paulin Chambers Company factory for over 100 years. In 1991, the factory closed and production of the iconic Canadian treat ceased. A couple from Camrose, Alberta revived the popular peanut-and-chocolate candy in 2018. Crystal Regehr Westergard, along with her husband Bert, started the comeback venture as a way to cheer up her 85-year-old mother by bringing her favourite candy to her nursing home. The term "Cuban lunch" came into use before WWI, during the Spanish-American War. A Cuban Lunch was a soldier's rectangular chocolate ration, and the slang term held on in common use.

The Bottle Houses

in Cape Egmont, Prince Edward Island was built in 1980 by Édouard Arsenault. It is a must-see tourist attraction that includes life-sized buildings made of over 25,000 recycled coloured glass bottles. The village contains a six-gabled house, a tavern, and a chapel in a tranquil garden setting. The Bottle Houses opens for the 2024 season on May 18th so add it to your summer travel list!

The
Lyric Theatre

is a performing arts theatre located in Swift Current, Saskatchewan. The building was declared a municipal heritage property in 2007. The theatre was originally built as a Vaudeville and silent film theatre with 400 seats. From 1918 to 1981, the theatre was operated as a movie theatre, but throughout its history has hosted a variety of events including orchestras, lectures, balls, church services, pet and poultry shows and dance classes, as well as serving as makeshift isolation quarters during the Spanish Flu epidemic of 1918.

Ice fishing is a popular winter pastime in Canada that is enjoyed on many Canadian lakes! Small holes are drilled through the frozen lake surfaces using ice augers, allowing for the fun activity of fishing during the winter months! Billed as the "Ice Fishing Capital of Canada," more people fish on Lake Simcoe, Ontario in winter than all other seasons combined.

is a Canadian non-profit organization dedicated to the promotion of careers in technology and skilled trades. One of their major initiatives is the annual Skills Canada National Competition. Hobby farms have become increasingly popular in Canada, especially among those looking to escape the hustle and bustle of city life. A hobby farm in Canada typically consists of a small acreage of land, usually between 5-10 acres, and is used to grow crops, raise animals, and/or provide a rural lifestyle. Other common activities on a hobby farm in Canada include beekeeping, keeping horses, and maintaining an orchard.

The members of the
Ceremonial Guard

are instantly recognizable in their distinctive scarlet tunics and bearskin caps. Although they come from many units across Canada, members of the Guard continue to uphold the traditions of two of Canada’s finest regiments - the Governor General’s Foot Guards (GGFG) of Ottawa, and the Canadian Grenadier Guards (CGG) of Montreal. They are easily discernable by the coloured plume in the bearskin hat: red for Foot Guards and white for Grenadier Guards. All of the bearskin hats worn by the Ceremonial Guard are more than 20 years old and are well-maintained from the thorough care they have received over the decades.

Edward Anton Maria Asselbergs (1927–1996) was a Dutch-Canadian food chemist famous for inventing the modern process of producing instant mashed potato flakes. He developed the process in 1960 while working for the Canadian Department of Agriculture in Ottawa. The product reached the market in 1962 and it was an instant success!

There are many iconic hotels in Canada known for their history, their cultural significance, and their incredible architecture, including  the Banff Springs Hotel, Château Frontenac, Château Laurier, Château Lake Louise, and the Empress Hotel, but there is only one hotel in Canada that is made entirely of snow and ice. The "
Hôtel de Glace

" (Ice Hotel) in Saint-Gabriel-de-Valcartier, Quebec is the oldest ice hotel in North America. Located just a short drive from Quebec City, this architectural marvel is a wonder to behold. Guests can explore the grand hall, an ice slide, a chapel, and a bar, where all furniture is made of ice and drinks are served in ice glasses.

Canada buys over 1.7 million boxes of KD each week, which is approximately 24% of what the company produces globally. On average, every Canadian eats more than three boxes of KD per year! KD is the most popular grocery item in Canada, and some consider it to be Canada’s true national dish.

Ovens Natural Park

is a privately owned 180-acre pristine reserve of coastal forest, located on the Atlantic coast of Nova Scotia, along the scenic Lighthouse Route near Lunenburg. For hundreds of years, people have been drawn to the incredible beauty, diverse geography, fascinating history, and spiritual solace that make The Ovens Natural Park so unique. Every year, thousands of park visitors hike the spectacular trails along the cliffs to view the famous sea caves, or "Ovens" for which the park was named.

is a 2023 documentary about a short kid from a Canadian army base that becomes the international pop culture darling of the 1980s - only to find the course of his life altered by a stunning diagnosis. The
Grouse Grind

in North Vancouver, British Columbia is a 2.5-kilometre trail up the face of Grouse Mountain, commonly referred to as "Mother Nature's Stairmaster". It is Vancouver's most used trail and is renowned for its challenge in requiring physical strength and endurance in order to make it to the top. The mountain was named in October 1894 by the first hikers to reach its peak, after the sooty (blue) grouse, a game bird of the area.

Many Canadians are unaware that western Canada lies along the Pacific "Ring of Fire" and has active volcanoes. In fact, there have been at least 49 volcanic eruptions in British Columbia and the Yukon Territory during the last 10,000 years. The most recent eruption in Canada took place at Lava Fork in northwestern B.C. about 150 years ago. Learn more about Canada's volcanoes on NRCan's
Volcanoes Canada

website.

The
Checkered Eye

is a wearable symbol that communicates its wearer is on the blindness spectrum. Created by Libby Thaw in Port Elgin, Ontario in 2000 in an effort to raise awareness about the spectrum of blindness and the symbols for blindness, the Checkered Eye is in use across Canada and around the world! The first full week in February is
White Cane Week

in Canada. Created in partnership by the Canadian National Institute for the Blind and the Canadian Council of the Blind (CCB) in 1946, this week not only educates the public about the challenges faced by those with visual impairments but also celebrates their resilience and ability to lead fulfilling lives.

Happy Lunar New Year! The Lunar New Year is an event celebrated by millions of people across the world on the first new moon of their calendar. It marks the beginning of the new year based on the lunar calendar or lunisolar calendar. It is one of the largest celebrations for Canada's Chinese population, and it is also celebrated by Canadians from Vietnam, Korea and Southeast Asia, among others.

The Smoke Eaters (aka Smokies) have existed as both junior and senior hockey teams in Canada since the 1920s. The senior Smoke Eaters won two Allan Cup championships, 1938 and 1962, and two Ice Hockey World Championships playing for Canada in 1939 and 1961. The senior Smokies were the last independent ice hockey club to represent Canada in international competition before the Canada men's national ice hockey team was established in 1963. The current Trail Smoke Eaters are a junior A ice hockey team from Trail, British Columbia, that are part of the British Columbia Hockey League. Learn more about the Smoke Eaters in

The province of Saskatchewan is known as the "Land of Living Skies" and even features the slogan on its provincial license plates.

Chalk River Laboratories

is a Canadian nuclear research facility in Deep River, about 180 km north-west of Ottawa. It is a site of major research and development to support and advance nuclear technology, particularly CANDU reactor technology. Until the shutdown of its nuclear reactor in 2018, CRL produced a large share of the world's supply of medical radioisotopes.

National Flag of Canada Day is observed every year on February 15. This day marks the inauguration of the present flag of Canada with the red maple leaf. The flag signifies the common values that unite all citizens of Canada - equality, diversity, and inclusion. Officially adopted as Canada's national flag in 1965, George Stanley's vision for Canada's flag was simple, easily recognizable and served as a rallying symbol for unity. 🇨🇦

Before Google, Bing, or Yahoo, there was the Archie Query which launched in 1990 as the world's first internet search engine. Archie, referred to as the "electronic index to the Internet," was developed by Alan Emtage, a postgraduate student and systems administrator at McGill University's School of Computer Science in Montreal, Quebec. The name Archie came from archive, without the "v".

The Farine Five Roses sign is a feature of the Montreal skyline, first erected above the Ogilvie flour mill in 1948. The unmistakeable symbol is over 75 years old, making it older than the Canadian flag. Its shining neon lights, which overlook the Lachine Canal, call out to Montreal drivers and pedestrians and remind them that they are, in fact, home.

A single game of snooker is called a frame. There was a time in the 1980s when Canadians were at the top of the snooker world, with three giants of the sport ranked in the top five spots: Kirk Stevens, "Big" Bill Webeniuk and Cliff Thorburn. In 1980, Thorburn defeated Alex Higgins 18 frames to 16 to win the prestigious World Snooker Championship, becoming the first non-British World Champion. Cliff Thorburn aka "The Grinder" was inducted into the Canada's Sports Hall of Fame in 2001.

Percé Rock is a huge sheer rock formation in the Gulf of Saint Lawrence on the tip of the Gaspé Peninsula in Quebec. Percé Rock appears from a distance like a ship under sail. It is one of the world's largest natural arches located in water and is considered a geologically and historically rich natural icon of Quebec.

The Cadborosaurus, affectionately known as "Caddy" by locals, is a sea serpent said to swim the waters of the northern Pacific; taking its name from Cadboro Bay in Greater Victoria, British Columbia. From early folklore to modern-day sailor tales, Caddy has become an enduring symbol of the often inexplicable and captivating nature of the sea. After the first sighting in 1933 in Oyster River, sightings of Caddy would continue for decades, although as time has gone on, sightings have been few and far between. Read more about Caddy in Craig Baird's Canadian History Ehx article:
The Sea Monster of Oyster River

.

The Canadian fishing village of Glover's Harbour, Newfoundland (formerly known as Thimble Tickle) claims to be the "Home of the Giant Squid." It earned this distinction in 1878, when a living giant squid was discovered stranded offshore. Now enshrined in the form of a "life-sized" sculpture, the world's largest squid is the subject of an annual festival, and in 2011, the sculpture was featured on a Canadian postage stamp.

Roderick George Toombs, better known as "Rowdy" Roddy Piper, was a Canadian professional wrestler and actor. Piper was best known to international audiences for his work with the World Wrestling Federation (now WWE) and World Championship Wrestling between 1984 and 2000. Although he was Canadian, Piper was billed as coming from Glasgow and was known for his signature kilt and bagpipe entrance music. In 2005, Piper was inducted into the WWE Hall of Fame by Ric Flair, who dubbed him "the most gifted entertainer in the history of professional wrestling".

The
Sopwith Camel

became the most successful British fighter of the First World War. Several Canadian aces used the Camel as their mount. Very manoeuvrable, it could be tricky to fly in the hands of a novice pilot. For experienced pilots, however, the aircraft proved to be a superb fighter. The guns were mounted on the forward fuselage with their breeches enclosed in a faired metal cowling—a "hump" that gave the Camel its name.

Canadian citizens have the civic duty to serve on a jury if called to do so and the civic responsibility to vote in Canadian elections. Happy International Women's Day! It's a global day to recognize and celebrate women's and girls' social, economic, cultural, and political achievements. It's also a time to raise awareness of the progress made towards achieving gender equality and the work remaining to be done. The 'Day of the Woman' was first proclaimed in 1910 at an international women’s conference in Copenhagen. However, it was only until the United Nations recognized the 8th of March as an official day of celebration in 1978. The Government of Canada's theme for
International Women's Day 2024

is "Invest in women: Accelerate progress". It's a call to action and a reminder that gender equality is one of the most effective ways to build healthier, more prosperous, and more inclusive communities. We all have a role to play in building a future where everyone can reach their full potential!
Wander through Vancouver Island's
Cathedral Grove in MacMillan Provincial Park

and you'll experience the unforgettable sight of some the oldest and tallest trees in Canada. The park's name will make perfect sense when you see sunlight filtering through the soaring 80-metre-high canopy. Plan to spend a few hours wandering Cathedral Grove's trail system and gazing up at 800-year-old giant Douglas Fir, Grand Firs, Western Hemlocks, and Western Red Cedar trees.

Commonwealth Day

Happy First Day of Spring! The mountain avens became the official floral emblem of the Northwest Territories in 1957. Part of the rose family, narrow leaves support tiny white flowers that bloom from June to July. Historically significant to the Inuit, the twisting of the seed head marked when to move inland and hunt caribou. It's one of the many wildflowers that signal the arrival of spring in the Arctic.

Montreal born
Leon Katz

was a pioneering biomedical engineer whose work significantly improved patient care and safety throughout Canada. Katz invented, innovated and hand-crafted a wide range of medical devices: Canada's first fetal heart monitor, a scanner-printer to detect radioactive iodine uptake in the thyroid gland, infant incubators, and a high-speed contrast injector for angiography. Notably, using his own original heart-lung bypass machine, he was the perfusionist for Canada’s first successful open heart surgery, and for thousands of subsequent operations.

Canada's political system is based on that of the United Kingdom. It is a constitutional monarchy, which means that we recognize the Queen or King as the Head of State, while the Prime Minister is the Head of Government. Currently, His Majesty King Charles III is King of Canada and Canada's Head of State. He is the personal embodiment of the Crown in Canada.

A state funeral for former Prime Minister Brian Mulroney is being held today in Montreal. Wayne Gretzky will be among the high-profile figures to eulogize the former PM. Mulroney passed away on Feb 29, 2024 at the age of 84. He served as the 18th prime minister of Canada from 1984 to 1993.

The ability to compress and transport hay and straw efficiently helped to open up new land for agriculture and settlement. The Canadian agricultural industry relies heavily on balers, with many farmers using them to harvest and process crops like wheat, barley, oats, and alfalfa.

Happy Easter from Canuckle! The
Vegreville egg

is a giant sculpture of a pysanka, a Ukrainian-style Easter egg, in the town of Vegreville, Alberta. Designed by Canadian artist Paul Maxym Sebaliu, the egg's shell is made up of an intricate set of two-dimensional anodized aluminum tiles in the shape of congruent equilateral triangles and star-shaped hexagons. The egg is 9 metres (31 feet) long and three and a half storeys high, weighing in at 2.5 tonnes (5,512 lb) making it the largest pysanka in the world! It was constructed in 1975 in honour of the early Ukrainian settlements east of Edmonton.

The Armstrong Whitworth Atlas was a British single-engine two-seat biplane. It served as an army co-operation aircraft for the Royal Canadian Air Force in the 1920s and 1930s. April 1, 2024 marked 100 years of service for the Royal Canadian Air Force as a distinct military element. The
RCAF Centennial

is an opportunity to showcase Air Force personnel and their accomplishments, demonstrate air and space power, enhance the reputation of the RCAF, and proudly honour its distinguished history and heritage.

Four species of cacti are native to Canada: pincushion cactus, little prickly pear cactus, common plains prickly pear cactus, and eastern prickly pear cactus. All of these species are at the northern limits of their distribution. In 1985, the Committee on the Status of Endangered Wildlife in Canada officially designated the cactus as an endangered plant.

On April 8, 2024, a spectacular and rare celestial event is set to unfold over Canada, the United States and Mexico – a
total solar eclipse

. As the Moon aligns perfectly between Earth and the Sun, temporary darkness will sweep across parts of the country, captivating countless spectators. In Canada, the solar eclipse's path of totality will pass through some cities and towns in Ontario, Quebec, New Brunswick, Nova Scotia, Prince Edward Island and Newfoundland, plunging them into darkness for a few minutes. People outside the path of totality will be able to observe a partial solar eclipse, during which the Sun is not hidden in totality.

Happy Total Solar Eclipse Day! Today, Canada will experience its first total solar eclipse since 1979. Only during a
total solar eclipse

, when the Moon blocks all of the Sun, does a smaller, darker conical shadow called the umbra project onto Earth. The width of the umbra (the path of totality) and the penumbra (the partial eclipse zone) depend on the moon's distance from Earth during the eclipse. The path of the umbra for today's total solar eclipse will be about 185 kilometres wide. All of Canada will see at least a partial solar eclipse, but parts of Ontario, Quebec and Atlantic Canada will experience totality.

Coast Salish peoples include a variety of First Nations living along the Northwest Pacific Coast in British Columbia. In traditional Coast Salish society, blankets held much importance. They were generally white twill-weave or twined featuring repeated geometric designs, and were thick and heavy. In addition to serving many practical functions, twill blankets were a primary medium of exchange and they held much spiritual significance.

William Leonard Hunt, also known by the stage name The Great Farini, was a well-known 19th- and early 20th-century Canadian funambulist, aka a tightrope walker extraordinaire. Aspen are a deciduous, hardwood tree of the genus Populus, belonging to the willow family. Trembling aspen and largetooth aspen are native to Canada; the former, found from the treeline to northern Mexico, is the most widely distributed tree in North America. Trembling aspen grows on most soils, doing best on well-drained, moist, sandy or gravelly loams. As a fast-growing pioneer tree species, it is ideal for quickly restoring a wide range of forest functions after disturbances.

After the decisive Battle of Vimy Ridge during the First World War, Canadian soldier, Lieutenant Leslie Miller, gathered acorns from oak trees amongst the rubble and wreckage. Miller brought the acorns back home to Scarborough, Ontario and planted the trees as a legacy to those who perished. The acorns from those trees were then repatriated and planted back in France in 2018. Lieutenant Leslie Miller's Vimy Oaks are now growing on Vimy Ridge, and today approximately 1,000 descendant Vimy Oaks have been planted across Canada as commemorative trees. Check out the
Vimy Oaks Legacy

website to learn more!

Air Canada Flight 143, commonly known as the
Gimli Glider

, was a Canadian scheduled domestic passenger flight between Montreal and Edmonton that ran out of fuel on Saturday, July 23, 1983. The flight crew successfully glided the Boeing 767 to an emergency landing at a former Royal Canadian Air Force base in Gimli, Manitoba, which had been converted to a racetrack, Gimli Motorsports Park. It resulted in no serious injuries to passengers or persons on the ground, and only minor damage to the aircraft. This unusual aviation incident earned the aircraft the nickname "Gimli Glider."

Red Dress Day

, also known as the National Day of Awareness for Missing and Murdered Indigenous Women and Girls and Two-Spirit People, is observed annually on May 5th. The day honours and brings awareness to the thousands of Indigenous women, girls and two-spirit people who have been subject to disproportionate violence in Canada. Red Dress Day was inspired by Métis artist Jaime Black’s REDress Project installation, in which she hung empty, red dresses to represent the missing and murdered women. Red dresses have become symbolic of the crisis as a result of her installation.

June is
National Indigenous History Month

in Canada, a time to recognize the rich history, heritage, resilience and diversity of First Nations, Inuit and Métis. One of the most frequent motifs in Coast Salish art was also a tool of high cultural importance. The spindle whorl allowed Salish women to weave beautiful textiles of spiritual and social significance. The spindle whorl was used to spin fleece into a thick yarn. It consisted of a small disk (whorl) with a shaft, inserted through a hole in the middle. The shaft was up to four feet long, or 120 cm, while the whorl was up to eight inches across, or 20 cm. The whorl was intricately carved from wood or stone with geometric, animal, or human designs. As the disk spun, the design would mesmerize the spinner, thereby bestowing special powers on the woven material.

The skilled model makers at Little Canada have meticulously recreated iconic Canadian sites such as Niagara Falls, the Parliament Buildings in Ottawa, and the Toronto skyline, down to the smallest detail. Visitors can marvel at the intricate craftsmanship and feel a sense of national pride as they explore these miniaturized versions of beloved Canadian locations. Little Canada offers a magical journey through the country, celebrating its rich heritage and stunning landscapes in a truly extraordinary way.

Cold Squad is a Canadian police television series that premiered on CTV on January 23, 1998, and ran for seven seasons. Known for its gripping stories and compelling characters, Cold Squad follows a team of homicide detectives from the Vancouver Police Department that reopen long-unsolved, or "cold" cases, using present-day forensic technology and psychological profiling to help crack them. Cold Squad was the first prime time national series produced out of Vancouver. With a total of 98 episodes, it became the longest-running prime time drama series on Canadian television and won several Gemini awards, Canada's highest television honours.

Canada is home to a unique maritime tradition known as the 'Blessing of the Fleet'! Each year in Digby, Nova Scotia, locals gather to participate in this special ceremony, which dates back over a century. The blessing of the scallop and deep sea fishing fleet is a time-honored tradition where fishermen and their vessels are blessed by clergy for a safe and bountiful fishing season. This event not only celebrates the rich maritime heritage of Nova Scotia but also serves as a symbol of community unity and support for those who make their living from the sea. It is a cherished tradition that brings together residents and visitors alike to offer prayers and well-wishes for the fishermen and their families.

Saddle Bronc event

at the Calgary Stampede showcases the skill and courage of riders as they strive to stay mounted on a bucking bronc for eight seconds, using only one hand. The cowboy holds on to a braided rein that is connected to the horse’s halter, using it to help balance himself. Riders are judged not only on their ability to stay on the horse but also on their style and control, making it a true test of horsemanship and athleticism. The Saddle Bronc event is a favourite among spectators and highlights the rich rodeo heritage of Canada.

Canada is home to some stunning coastal landscapes adorned with picturesque capes! Along its vast coastline, Canada boasts numerous capes that offer breathtaking views of the surrounding seas and landscapes. A ketch is a two-masted sailboat whose mainmast is taller than the mizzen mast, and whose mizzen mast is stepped forward of the rudder post. Oriole celebrated her 100th anniversary in 2021 by making a series of port visits in Atlantic Canada.

Did you know that a group of lynx is called a "chain"? The Canada lynx, a native species to Canada, is known for its distinctive tufted ears and large, padded paws that act like snowshoes in the winter. These elusive cats are most commonly found in the boreal forests of Canada, where they primarily hunt snowshoe hares. While lynx are typically solitary animals, the term "chain" is used to describe a group of them when they do come together. Seeing a chain of lynx in the wild is a rare and remarkable sight, reflecting the rich biodiversity of Canada's wilderness.

! Acadian food is part of Canada's French heritage, and it's got a distinct history and flavours which are intertwined with neighbouring Québec. Fried clams or "coques frites" is a delicious traditional Acadian dish enjoyed by many in Canada's Maritime provinces. With an abundance of fresh seafood, there's no better place to dig in to some battered fried clams along with a side of crispy fries. You'll be happy as a clam when you try this dish!

Did you know that a group of jellyfish is called a "smack"? In Canada, jellyfish can be found along the Atlantic, Pacific, and Arctic coasts. One of the most notable species is the Lion's Mane jellyfish, which is commonly seen in the colder waters off the coast of Nova Scotia and Newfoundland. These jellyfish can have tentacles that extend over 30 metres, making them one of the largest jellyfish species in the world. Encountering a smack of jellyfish is a mesmerizing experience, showcasing the diversity of marine life in Canadian waters.

The onion is Canada's most important condiment crop. It is a biennial, herbaceous plant of the Amaryllidaceae family. In 2022, the annual Canadian production of dry onions was over 287,000 metric tons, worth approximately $157.2 million. Dry onions are mainly produced in Quebec (46%), Ontario (37%) and Alberta (10%). There is even a species of onion called the Canada onion (Allium canadense) that is native to eastern North America.

, Saskatchewan is home to the world's largest bunnock! A bunnock is an anklebone of a horse, and is used in a game of the same name. Within the 9.8-metre high fibre-glass horse anklebone replica, there is also a tourist information booth. Every year since 1992, Macklin hosts a world championship bunnock tournament that brings out over 1,000 competitors each August long weekend, doubling the town's population.

Did you know that Canada played a significant role in the production of military jeeps during World War II? The Willys MB, commonly known as the 'jeep,' was a versatile vehicle used by Allied forces for various military purposes, including transportation, reconnaissance, and even combat. Canadian factories, such as the Ford Motor Company of Canada in Windsor, Ontario, were instrumental in manufacturing these iconic vehicles to support the war effort. In fact, Canada produced over 100,000 military jeeps during the war, contributing to the Allied victory. Today, the jeep remains a symbol of Canadian wartime manufacturing prowess and innovation.

Red Osier Dogwood is a common shrub found in every province and territory in Canada. It is a favourite shrub among gardeners and landscapers because it is a showpiece all year long. In the summer it is lush green in colour while in the fall it has foliage of reds and oranges. In the wild, the Red Osier Dogwood is commonly found growing along the edges of swamps, ponds, and streams.

Did you know that yodelling has deep roots in Canadian music? Iconic Canadian country singer Hank Snow delighted audiences in the 1930s and 1940s with his distinctive yodelling style and he was nicknamed The Yodelling Ranger. Donn Reynolds, another Canadian yodeller, set a world record by yodelling non-stop for an incredible 7 hours and 29 minutes in 1976. He also achieved the fastest 5-tone yodel in just 1.9 seconds in 1984. Stompin' Tom Connors, a beloved Canadian folk singer, also incorporated yodelling into his music, adding a unique touch to his memorable performances. These artists highlight the rich tradition of yodelling in Canada, showcasing the country's diverse musical heritage.

Cloudberries are a member of the Rosaceae family and are a relative of the raspberry. These amber berries are a rare find as they are native to cool temperate regions, alpine and Arctic tundra and boreal forest. Cloudberries are also known as bakeapples or bake-apple berries in Atlantic Canada. This herbaceous perennial produces amber-colored edible fruit similar to the blackberry.

Schmoo torte is a Canadian dessert specialty that originated in Winnipeg, Manitoba. A classic schmoo torte consists of layers of sponge cake or angel food cake, whipped cream, nuts, and caramel sauce. The schmoo torte was first invented in 1948 by a Winnipeg mother, Dora Zaslavsky, for her son Murray's Bar Mitzvah.

The Littlest Hobo is a Canadian television series from the 1960s and 1980s based upon a well-known 1958 movie of the same name. The concept of the show was that of "an ownerless dog" - the titular "Hobo" - an extremely intelligent stray German Shepherd who wanders from town to town helping people in need. But did you know that there were actually several "Hobos" who shared the spotlight? The leading canine was played by multiple dogs who would alternate during the filming of the series. The primary star was named London, but a number of London's relatives, including Toro, Litlon, and Thorn also played scenes as the Hobo.

Every day, the Royal Artillery fires the noon gun at the
Halifax Citadel National Historic Site

in Halifax, Nova Scotia. The 19th-century British fort fires one of the oldest continuous noon guns in the world. The cannon's blast has been part of Halifax's daily rituals since 1857. The present noon gun is a reproduction 12 pounder, smooth-bore muzzle loading gun used during the reign of King George III (1760-1820).

Canada has some amazing areas, trails, and parks you can walk, but did you know there are a couple that are among the world's happiest walks? Inghams, a UK travel company that specializes in active holidays around the lakes and mountains of Europe and Canada, looked at social media posts taken on popular walking trails around the world and used facial detection software to find and compare the size of the smiles captured in the posts, generating a happiness score and providing a tangible measurement of how happy travellers are as they embark on different walks around the world! The World's Happiest Walks

', with the Bruce Trail in Tobermory, Ontario and the Canadian Rockies in BC & Alberta coming in with a happiness score of 93.67!

Canada is home to some stunning oxbow features! An oxbow is a crescent-shaped lake or bend that forms when a river's meander gets cut off from the main flow. The
town of Oxbow

, Saskatchewan is named after the oxbow-shaped bend in the nearby river. These unique landforms are often found in the floodplains of Canadian rivers and are known for their picturesque beauty and rich biodiversity. Oxbows provide important habitats for wildlife and offer great opportunities for outdoor activities like fishing and birdwatching.

Did you know that Canada has a rich history of producing iconic comic strips? One of the most famous Canadian comic strips is 'For Better or For Worse' created by Lynn Johnston. This heartwarming comic strip, which debuted in 1979, follows the daily lives of the Patterson family and addresses relatable themes such as family dynamics, relationships, and social issues. '

Perched in the heart of Huron at the junction of Highways 4 and 8 is the iconic radar antenna that has forever identified Clinton, Ontario as '
the Home of Radar

' in Canada. The antenna is part of the AN/FPS-20 long range radar system and is a relic of the Cold War. It was originally donated by the Canadian Forces Base Clinton in 1967 in honour of the Canadian Centennial as a monument to the country and the people trained at the base. The base was Canada's first radar school, opening in August 1941, and the site trained nearly 9,000 students by the end of World War II: more than 6,500 Canadians and over 2,300 Americans.

On the
Extension Ridge Trail

in Nanaimo, British Columbia, hikers can find what's known as the Abyss. It is essentially a long crevice in the ground, stretching about 40 metres long and 16" wide. This interesting geological feature is surrounded by arbutus trees, which add to its natural beauty. The Abyss is a large fissure or crack in the earth and was most likely formed thousands of years ago due to seismic shifts. To date there is no information about how deep the crack is. It's definitely a unique geological formation one should see!

Lobster boils are a beloved culinary tradition on Canada's East Coast! Lobster boils are a festive occasion where friends and family gather to enjoy fresh lobster, along with an array of other seafood and seasonal delights. As the lobsters cook to perfection, the aroma fills the air, creating an atmosphere of anticipation and excitement. Once ready, the succulent lobster meat is served alongside melted butter, corn on the cob, potatoes, and other accompaniments, creating a feast fit for any maritime celebration.

While 'terry' can refer to a type of soft, absorbent fabric, it also carries special meaning in Canada as the name of Terry Fox, a true Canadian hero whose Marathon of Hope continues to inspire millions. September 16 is
World Ozone Day
! Canada is known for its commitment to environmental conservation, including efforts to protect the ozone layer through initiatives such as the
Montreal Protocol

. The Montreal Protocol has succeeded in halting the damage to the ozone layer and is helping the world in the battle against climate change. In Canada and around the world, the Montreal Protocol regulates the production and consumption of numerous substances that deplete the ozone layer, the Earth’s atmospheric shield that prevents UV radiation from harming humans and other forms of life. It is considered one of the most successful, global environmental agreements of all time.

Whether it's maintaining tall buildings in bustling cities like Toronto or Vancouver, the bosun's chair is a vital tool that ensures safety while getting the job done.

Canada is home to some spectacular natural caves waiting to be explored! While Canada may be known for its vast wilderness and towering mountains, it also boasts a hidden underground world filled with breathtaking caverns and geological wonders. Whether you're spelunking through narrow passageways or admiring dazzling rock formations, exploring caves in Canada is an unforgettable adventure that reveals the hidden beauty beneath the surface of the Great White North.

Located in Kingston, Ontario,
Shoal Tower

is one of four Martello towers built in the mid-19th century to defend the entrance to Kingston Harbour and the Royal Naval Dockyard. These towers were part of a network of fortifications constructed during the tensions between Canada and the United States, particularly during the War of 1812 and its aftermath. Shoal Tower, constructed on a submerged shoal in Lake Ontario, served as a strategic point of defense against potential enemy attacks from the lake. Today, Shoal Tower stands as a testament to Canada's military history and is open to visitors interested in exploring its historic significance and architectural design.

Sam Bat

, officially The Original Maple Bat Corporation, is a Canadian company based in the town of Carleton Place, Ontario that manufactures baseball bats. It was the first company to supply baseball bats manufactured from maple wood to professional baseball players. Loved by baseball players at all levels worldwide, the legendary Sam Bat was used by Barry Bonds to to break the world record for both most homers in a season and a career. The records continued with Miguel Cabrera using a Sam Bat to win the Triple Crown in 2012, and Giancarlo Stanton using a Sam Bat to win the 2016 Home Run Derby.

Every fall, Canada's rivers and streams come alive with the incredible sight of salmon spawning! These resilient fish travel upstream, often covering hundreds of kilometres, to lay their eggs in the same waters where they were born. British Columbia's Fraser River is one of the most famous spawning grounds in the world, where millions of sockeye salmon return each year. This natural cycle is crucial to the ecosystem, supporting wildlife from eagles to bears. Watching the salmon spawn is a quintessential Canadian experience and a celebration of nature's perseverance!

Canada's diverse geology is famous for its wealth of minerals and precious stones, often found in geodes - those mysterious-looking rocks that, when cracked open, reveal beautiful crystals.

was a distinguished Canadian geologist known for his groundbreaking contributions to mineral exploration and as a godfather of volcanic massive sulfide (VMS) research in Canada. His work helped shape modern mining practices in Canada, making him a pivotal figure in the country's resource exploration and development. Franklin's legacy in Canadian geology is a reminder of the important role geoscience plays in our everyday lives, from mining to environmental stewardship.

John Deighton, better known as "Gassy Jack", was a bar-owner in British Columbia. The Gastown neighbourhood of Vancouver takes its name from him. Deighton was known as Gassy Jack because of his talkative nature and his penchant for storytelling. A statue of Gassy Jack was installed in Gastown where Deighton had built the Globe Saloon in 1867, one of the first buildings in Vancouver. Although Deighton is one of Vancouver's founding figures, he is considered a controversial character for several reasons tied to both his personal life and his role in the city's colonial past. In 2022, protesters tore down the statue of Gassy Jack during the Women's Memorial March.

During World War II, over 7,000 women proudly served in the
Women's Royal Canadian Naval Service

(WRCNS), affectionately known as the Wrens. Established in 1942, these trailblazing women supported Canada's war effort by taking on critical roles in communications, intelligence, and logistics - paving the way for greater inclusion of women in the military. The WRCNS was disbanded in 1946, but their legacy continues to be celebrated today, with many Remembrance Day ceremonies honouring their service alongside the men and women of the Canadian Armed Forces. Their dedication and resilience are a testament to the important role women have played in shaping Canada's military history. As we remember and honour our veterans, the contributions of the Wrens should never be forgotten.

On November 11, Canadians commemorate Remembrance Day by wearing a red poppy on their lapels to honour military personnel who served and sacrificed in conflicts. The poppy is worn on the left side, close to the heart, symbolizing respect and remembrance. Inspired by the iconic World War I poem "In Flanders Fields" by Lieutenant Colonel John McCrae, this tradition of wearing poppies began in 1921, and the phrase "lest we forget" serves as a powerful reminder of the importance of remembering those who served Canada. This simple yet significant act of wearing a poppy reminds us all of the courage and sacrifice of past generations and honours their memory with deep reverence.

The foyer's lower level contains sculptures that depict themes of art, history and philosophy. In the upper level, the stonework shows the coat of arms of each Canadian province. The foyer also contains framed portraits of Canada's prime ministers, contributing to the foyer's dignified character.

During the First World War, a Canadian innovation helped protect soldiers from toxic gases on the battlefield. The
Hypo Helmet

, invented by Canadian doctor Captain Cluny Macpherson in 1915, was an early type of gas mask designed to filter out toxic gases like chlorine gas. It was made of a chemically treated flannel hood with a mica visor, and was a crucial development in protecting soldiers from the harmful effects of chemical warfare. This Canadian invention was an important step in the evolution of gas masks, helping to save countless lives during the war.

Tomorrow (November 27th) marks the 100th anniversary of the Civic campus of
The Ottawa Hospital

. The original red brick hospital was built in 1924 and was the dream of Harold Fisher, Ottawa's mayor at the time, who envisioned a state-of-the-art building that would bring together three community hospitals struggling under an aging infrastructure and a deadly influenza pandemic. Now, a century later, on the heels of another pandemic, a $500-million fundraising campaign is underway to help build a new hospital campus right in the heart of the very community where Mayor Fisher first dared to dream.

There are 3 endemic venomous snake species in Canada that fall within the pit viper family: the Western rattlesnake, the Prairie rattlesnake, and the Eastern Massasauga rattlesnake. These pit vipers are equipped with heat-sensing pits that help them hunt small mammals even in the cooler Canadian climate. Though they might seem intimidating, all three rattlesnakes are shy creatures that avoid human contact and play an essential role in controlling rodent populations. Conservation efforts, particularly for the endangered Eastern Massasauga, help protect these unique members of Canada's wildlife. So, next time you think of "viper," remember these important native species, quietly helping keep Canada's ecosystems balanced!

The Northern Pygmy Owl, one of Canada's smallest and most elusive birds of prey, is a true example of how big things can come in small packages. Despite its diminutive size - only about the size of a sparrow - this tiny owl is a fierce hunter, preying on small mammals and birds. Found in the forests of British Columbia and the Yukon, the Northern Pygmy Owl is known for its bold, yellow eyes that can give it a fierce look despite its small stature. Its size, combined with its powerful hunting skills, makes it a remarkable part of Canada’s birdlife.

Located in Timmins, Ontario, the
Kidd Creek Mine

is the world's deepest base-metal mine below sea level, with shaft bottom at 3014 metres.  Operating as the deepest operating metalliferous mine in the world, the mine has multiple winzes which connect the different levels. A winze is like a shaft that starts underground. Kidd Creek's depth is more than five times the height of Toronto's CN Tower!

Knolls are found throughout Canada, and the term generally refers to small, rounded hills or mounds that often create scenic viewpoints or unique natural features in the landscape. Knolls can also be found in more northern areas of Canada where glacial activity historically shaped the terrain, leaving behind knolls, drumlins, and other geological features.

The Axial Seamount, an undersea volcano in the Pacific Ocean, influences the west coast's marine ecosystem. The Axial Seamount may sit off the coast of the United States, but it's still part of a unique underwater volcanic chain that Canada helps study! This active submarine volcano lies along the Juan de Fuca Ridge, a tectonic boundary just west of Vancouver Island. Canada's involvement in ocean research, particularly through programs like
Ocean Networks Canada

, helps monitor activity in the Axial Seamount and other geological features in the Pacific Ocean. The insights from this research help scientists understand undersea volcanic activity and potential impacts on the Canadian coast.

Canada's eastern coastlines, including the Gulf of St. Lawrence and the waters off Newfoundland and Labrador, are vital whelping grounds for harp seals each winter. Whelping, the term for giving birth to pups, takes place on the sea ice, which provides a safe environment for harp seal mothers to nurse their newborns. These iconic seals migrate south from the Canadian Arctic every fall, relying on stable ice conditions to raise their pups. The fluffy white coats of harp seal pups are a familiar sight in these regions, making whelping season a unique and cherished part of Canada’s Arctic and marine ecosystem.

Did you know the humble zipper owes its "teeth" to Canadian ingenuity? Though small, this invention would go on to revolutionize fashion, camping gear, and much more. Next time you zip up, give a nod to Canada's history of clever creations!

During the Klondike Gold Rush, Kathleen "Klondike Kate" Rockwell, affectionately known as "Kitty," became one of the Yukon's most celebrated entertainers. Drawn by tales of fortune, Kitty arrived in Dawson City in 1900 and quickly found fame as a dancer. She dazzled packed saloons with her vaudeville routines, including her signature "Flame Dance," which transformed her red sequined dress and flowing chiffon into a fiery spectacle. A symbol of resilience and charm, Kitty brought joy and excitement to the often grueling lives of miners who had flocked to the north in search of gold. Kitty's name remains synonymous with the Klondike spirit, capturing the allure and larger-than-life characters that defined one of Canada's most legendary eras.

Looking for the biggest jewel in Canada? The town of Black Diamond, Alberta, is home to the world's largest black diamond! This striking monument was unveiled during the town's centennial Jubilee in 2007, paying homage to its namesake and rich coal mining history. In the Canadian military, "Bravo" is more than just an exclamation of praise; it's the code word for the letter "B" in the NATO phonetic alphabet, which ensures clear communication across the Canadian Armed Forces and beyond. The phonetic alphabet - from "Alpha" to "Zulu" - is used worldwide, helping Canadian military personnel coordinate and reduce misunderstandings during missions. Bravo is also commonly used to name companies within various units like the Bravo Company, 1st Battalion, The Royal Canadian Regiment (1 RCR)  Battle Group. Additionally, the term Bravo Zulu (BZ) is also a naval signal meaning "well done", and is commonly used across all branches to commend service members for a job well executed.

David Thompson was a legendary map maker, earning him the title "the Stargazer" or "Koo-Koo-Sint" among some Indigenous peoples. An Anglo-Canadian fur trader, explorer, and surveyor, Thompson travelled an astonishing 90,000 kilometres across North America during his career, meticulously mapping 4.9 million square kilometres of the continent. His unparalleled work in cartography has led many to call him the "greatest practical land geographer that the world has produced." These maps not only shaped the understanding of North America's vast landscapes but also reflected his collaboration with Indigenous communities, whose knowledge greatly influenced his work.

Canada's most unique terrestrial animal is probably not what you might think. A recent study published in the journal
The Canadian Field-Naturalist

has found the country's most evolutionarily distinct Canadian animal is the spiny softshell turtle! These turtles can be found in Ontario and Quebec, and are one of the largest freshwater turtle species in North America. But unfortunately, they have been listed as one of Canada's rarest species for over 20 years. If we let them disappear from Canada, we would lose almost 180 million years of unique evolutionary history. This shows how important it is to prioritize the conservation of this species and its unique legacy amongst Canadian wildlife.

Their legacy was cemented with their induction into the Ultimate Canada Hall of Fame in 2014. Ultimate Frisbee thrives in Canada, where sportsmanship and skill go hand in hand - making Chaos's achievements all the more fitting!

In Westlock, Alberta, you can find a truly unique roadside attraction: the world's largest working weather vane. This 15-metre-tall structure features a massive tractor perched on top, paying homage to the region's agricultural roots. Weather vanes, which are used to indicate wind direction, are common across Canada, often found on barns and buildings, but the one in Westlock stands out for its size and creativity. It's a symbol of both the farming heritage and the playful spirit of the Canadian countryside. Fittingly, the weather vane sits just outside of the
Canadian Tractor Museum

Whelks are spiral-shelled sea snails commonly found in the cold waters off Canada's Atlantic coast. These hardy creatures are not only a key part of marine ecosystems but also an important catch in the fishing industry, where they are harvested for their delicate meat and exported worldwide. Indigenous peoples have long valued whelks, using their shells to create tools, jewellery, and even decorative wampum beads. These small but mighty molluscs are a fascinating part of Canada's coastal heritage!

Natural Resources Canada has developed a national
Earthquake Early Warning

(EEW) system designed to provide early detection and warning in areas of moderate to high earthquake hazard and with concentrations of population and infrastructure. In Canada, this earthquake risk is concentrated in western British Columbia, eastern Ontario, and southern Quebec. The EEW system began operation in BC in the spring of 2024 and will begin in Quebec and Ontario in early 2025.

Canadian pair skaters Deanna Stellato-Dudek and Maxime Deschamps made history at the 2024 World Figure Skating Championships in Montreal by clinching the gold medal in the pairs event. Their exceptional performance captivated audiences and judges alike, earning them top marks and solidifying their status as world champions. Their victory marked the first world title for a Canadian pair since 2016, highlighting their remarkable skill, grace, and athleticism on the ice.

Sandbanks Provincial Park

Henry Ruttan was a prominent businessman, inventor, and politician in Upper Canada who revolutionized railway travel. In 1858, he patented an innovative system for heating and cooling railway coaches, which was adopted by several railway companies across North America. His system used the motion of the train to force outside air through ducts and a wash tank, where the air was cleaned, humidified, and cooled. During winter, the system was adapted to heat the air entering the cars, making train travel more comfortable year-round. Ruttan's invention was a game-changer for passenger comfort and a brilliant example of Canadian ingenuity!

Canada experienced "Trudeaumania" in the late 1960s and early 1970s, a wave of excitement sparked by Pierre Trudeau, the charismatic leader of the Liberal Party. With his progressive policies, youthful energy, and charm, Trudeau became a symbol of change and modernity during a transformative period in Canadian history. His rise to power as Prime Minister in 1968 captivated Canadians from coast to coast. "Trudeaumania" saw fans attending packed rallies, sporting Trudeau-inspired fashion, and collecting buttons and memorabilia. This phenomenon left a lasting impact on Canadian politics and culture, marking a unique and spirited chapter in the nation's political history.

In Canada, marshy areas known as sedges are important habitats for wildlife, especially for cranes! The term "sedge" not only refers to grassy wetlands but also to a group of cranes gathering in these lush areas. Canada is home to two species of cranes - the Sandhill Crane and the endangered Whooping Crane. Each fall, Sandhill Cranes flock to Canadian wetlands, where sedges provide the perfect mix of cover and food as they prepare for migration. These crane gatherings are a beautiful sight, showcasing how Canada's diverse ecosystems support unique wildlife year-round.

A tuya is a rare type of volcano with a flat top and steep sides, formed when lava erupts through a thick glacier or ice sheet. These unique formations are found in regions where glaciers and volcanic activity occurred simultaneously. One of the most notable tuya regions is the Tuya Volcanic Field in far northern British Columbia, near the border with the Yukon. This area, centered around the Tuya Range, showcases some of the best examples of tuyas in the world.

Did you know Canada has an island that literally shakes? Tremble Island, located in British Columbia's Seymour Inlet, experiences a phenomenon where the ground quivers due to the power of the water currents surrounding it. This inlet has some of the fastest tidal surges in the world, and at peak tides, the force of the water is so intense that it causes the entire island to tremble! Visitors to Tremble Island are in for a unique (and slightly shaky) experience, showcasing the raw power of Canada's incredible natural landscapes.

in southwestern Saskatchewan are a breathtaking mix of rugged beauty and rich history. This hidden gem offers visitors a chance to explore Saskatchewan’s dramatic geography and learn about the deep cultural significance of the land to Indigenous peoples. It's a truly muddy marvel waiting to be discovered!

Lumberjacks hold a legendary place in Canadian history, pivotal to the booming timber industry of the 19th and early 20th centuries. Among the most celebrated was Joseph "Jos" Montferrand, a French-Canadian logger and strongman from Quebec. Montferrand earned folk hero status for his remarkable strength and his fearless defence of fellow workers, and was the inspiration for the legendary Ottawa Valley figure Big Joe Mufferaw. Today, the enduring image of the plaid-clad lumberjack with an axe is a proud symbol of Canada's rugged and resilient heritage.

In Canada, a reeve is the head of a rural or small-town municipal council, similar to a mayor. The role dates back to the early days of local governance when reeves presided over townships to oversee taxes, infrastructure, and community concerns. While fewer communities use the term today, reeves remain an important part of rural governance in some provinces, connecting modern leadership to Canada's history. Of course, the most famous of all the reeves in Canada is actor Keanu Reeves - but he enforces justice in a very different way!

Did you know that the RCMP's red serge tunic is a symbol of Canadian pride and history? Introduced in the 1870s, the red tunic was part of the uniform for the North-West Mounted Police, the RCMP's predecessor. The bold colour was chosen for its high visibility and as a symbol of authority and strength. Over time, the red serge became a lasting emblem of the RCMP's commitment to service and law enforcement across Canada. Today, it remains an iconic part of their ceremonial dress and is instantly recognized worldwide as a representation of Canada's national identity.

Canada's early internet networks relied on being wired to telephone lines and later advanced to fibre optics, connecting the nation's vast geography. In 1984, NetNorth, Canada's first digital network, linked universities using dial-up lines, allowing researchers to exchange data across provinces. This wired system laid the foundation for CA*net in 1990, Canada's first national internet backbone, which revolutionized communication and collaboration across the country. From these humble beginnings, Canada's internet infrastructure has grown into one of the most advanced in the world!

While Canada's internet has come a long way, there's still work to be done to connect all Canadians to quality, accessible internet - especially those in rural and remote communities. To help you understand your internet connection and bridge the digital divide, our friends at CIRA released a new and improved version of their Internet Performance Test (IPT). Every CIRA IPT run helps provide internet researchers and municipalities with an understanding of the state of Canada's internet and where connectivity gaps exist.
