The Canadian Football League (CFL) once had two teams with nearly identical names: the Ottawa Rough Riders and the Saskatchewan Roughriders. This led to some confusion among fans and broadcasters, especially during games between the two teams. In 1996, the Ottawa team folded, leaving Saskatchewan as the sole "Roughriders" in the CFL. The Ottawa franchise returned in 2002 as the Ottawa Renegades, and later became the Ottawa Redblacks in 2014. Despite the name changes, the legacy of the "Rough Riders" and "Roughriders" remains a unique chapter in Canadian football history.

Canada is one of the top producers of pork in the world, with millions of pigs raised across the country, especially in provinces like Quebec, Ontario, and Manitoba. Canadian pork is a popular export, found on dinner tables across the globe! On Super Bowl Sunday, pigs make an appearance in more ways than one! While you’re enjoying pulled pork sliders or crispy bacon,  it's interesting to note that the term "pigskin" for footballs originates from the early days of the sport. Originally, footballs were made from inflated pig bladders, which served as the ball's core. These bladders were encased in leather to form the ball. This "pigskin" ball was standard for football for many years before modern synthetic materials took over.

The Common Five-lined Skink is one of Canada's native lizard species. Found in Ontario along the southern edge of the Canadian Shield, these small, speedy reptiles prefer warm, rocky habitats and forested areas. Juveniles are especially eye-catching with their bright blue tails, which they can detach to escape predators - a clever survival trick! While their tails eventually regrow, it takes a lot of energy, so they don't drop them unless absolutely necessary. As a cold-blooded species, skinks bask in the sun to stay warm, making them a rare but fascinating sight in the Canadian wild!

The Oak Island mystery is a series of stories and legends concerning buried treasure and unexplained objects found on or near Oak Island in Nova Scotia. Since the 18th century, attempts have been made to find treasure and artifacts. Hypotheses about artifacts present on the island range from pirate treasure to Shakespearean manuscripts to the Holy Grail or the Ark of the Covenant. A "curse" on the treasure is said to have originated more than a century ago and states that seven men will die in the search for the treasure before it is found. This intriguing tale has been the subject of the popular TV series "The Curse of Oak Island," which follows brothers Rick and Marty Lagina as they explore the island's mysteries.

As we celebrate 60 years of this iconic emblem, Canadians continue to hoist their flag high with pride from coast to coast to coast.

An edict is a formal announcement or decree that establishes a law or order. Often associated with monarchies, edicts have been used throughout history to communicate official orders, mandates, and laws. In Canada, royal edicts played a key role in shaping governance and establishing provinces under the Crown. The Quebec Act of 1774, an edict from King George III, protected French civil law and religious rights, helping to preserve Quebec's distinct identity. Even today, Canada remains a constitutional monarchy, with royal proclamations continuing to mark significant national events.

On December 31, 1966, the people of Bowsman, Manitoba marked the start of Canada's Centennial Year in a fiery way. To celebrate their town finally getting sewer service, residents set their old outhouses (or "biffies") ablaze in what became known as the Biffy Burn. It was a fitting farewell to an era of backyard biffies - and a warm welcome to indoor plumbing! Bowsman's postal code was changed to R0L 0H0 which stands for "Roll Our Loyal Out Houses Over" in honour of this event.

The
Capilano Suspension Bridge

in Vancouver, British Columbia has been a popular destination for adventurers since the late 1890s. Back then, reaching the bridge was no easy feat. Hikers had to first cross the Burrard Inlet by steamboat, then trek through a 12km path winding through dense rainforest to get to the bridge. Those who made the long journey were said to 'tramp' their way to the bridge and were dubbed 'Capilano Tramps', earning the nickname for their determination and endurance. Today, visitors still experience the beauty of the bridge, but thankfully, they don’t have to tramp their way through the wilderness to get there!

Sable Island

, a remote sandbar about 300 km southeast of Halifax, is famous for its wild horses, which have roamed free for centuries! These resilient animals have adapted to the island's harsh conditions, grazing on its unusual vegetation. Sable Island is also known as the "Graveyard of the Atlantic," with over 350 recorded shipwrecks scattered along its shifting shores. Today, it is a protected National Park Reserve, with visitor access carefully managed by Parks Canada to help safeguard its delicate ecosystem. Even trees don't thrive here - except for a single Scots pine, planted in the 1960s, standing as the island's only tree!

In the iconic
Heritage Minute about the Canadian Pacific Railway

, titled 'Nitro,' a young Chinese railway worker bravely risks his life to set a dangerous nitroglycerine charge, helping carve a path through the rugged Canadian Rockies in the 1880s. His actions are a powerful symbol of the perilous work undertaken by thousands of Chinese labourers who played a vital role in building Canada's first transcontinental railway. Between 1880 and 1885, over 15,000 Chinese workers toiled under hazardous conditions, constructing some of the most challenging sections of the railway in British Columbia.

Flooding

stands as Canada's most frequent and expensive natural disaster. In fact, floods account for 40% of weather-related disasters in the country! From spring ice jams in the Red River Valley to coastal storm surges in the Maritimes, flooding can impact communities across the country. Some of Canada's most significant floods include the 2013 Alberta floods, which caused billions in damages, and the 1997 "Flood of the Century" in Manitoba. With climate change increasing extreme weather events, Canada continues to invest in flood prevention to keep communities safe from rising waters.

Pemmican is a nutrient-rich food made from dried meat, tallow, and sometimes dried berries. Traditionally prepared by Indigenous Peoples in parts of North America, it has long been valued for its long shelf life and high energy content, making it an essential food for survival, particularly during long journeys across Canada's vast landscapes. Pemmican can be eaten raw or used in prepared meals, and it remains part of Indigenous cuisine today. The word pemmican comes from the Cree word 'pimikan,' meaning 'manufactured grease.'

A skarn is a type of metamorphic rock that forms when hot magma interacts with surrounding limestone or dolostone. This contact creates new minerals, often rich in metals like copper, gold, and tungsten. In Canada, skarn deposits have been significant in mining areas like British Columbia and Quebec, where valuable ores are extracted. Skarns highlight how geological processes transform rocks into mineral-rich resources that play an important role in the mining industry!

A xeric habitat is one with very little moisture, creating dry, desert-like conditions. While Canada is known for its forests and snowy winters, it also has xeric ecosystems! The South Okanagan in British Columbia is home to one of the country's most well-known dry regions, featuring sagebrush, cacti, and even rattlesnakes. Other xeric areas include the badlands of Alberta, the Carcross Desert in Yukon, and even polar deserts in the High Arctic, where precipitation is minimal. In the Prairies, sandy dunes like those in Saskatchewan's Great Sand Hills also support drought-resistant plants. These unique ecosystems are home to species specially adapted to thrive in dry conditions.

April is National Poetry Month in Canada - a time to celebrate the power of words and the beauty of verse! An elegy is a type of poem that expresses sorrow or reflection, often written in tribute to someone who has passed. One of Canada's most famous elegies is In Flanders Fields, written by Lieutenant-Colonel John McCrae during the First World War. This moving poem, penned in 1915, continues to resonate as a powerful symbol of remembrance and Canadian literary strength.

Whether for sport or relaxation, skiffs are an integral part of Canada's connection to the water!

Happy
Earth Day

! Did you know that Canada's veteran satellite, SCISAT, has been orbiting Earth 15 times a day for over 21 years? It's the only satellite in the world that can monitor all the regulated gases covered by the Montreal Protocol—an international agreement to protect the ozone layer. SCISAT helps scientists around the globe track the impact of over 70 gases in our atmosphere, playing a vital role in efforts to reduce harmful emissions and combat climate change.

The word pekan comes from the Algonquian name for the fisher, a clever and elusive member of the weasel family found throughout Canada's forests. Despite its name, the pekan doesn't fish - instead, it's one of the few predators that can take down a porcupine! Known for its dark fur and tree-climbing skills, the pekan plays an important role in keeping forest ecosystems balanced. Its name lives on in places like the Pekan Trail in Quebec, showing how Indigenous languages continue to shape the map of Canada.

For over 5,500 years, Indigenous Peoples of the Plains used a strategic chase to guide bison toward cliffs known as buffalo jumps. At
Head-Smashed-In Buffalo Jump

in Alberta, hunters built stone cairns to funnel herds toward a steep drop.  Now a UNESCO World Heritage Site with a museum dedicated to Blackfoot culture, it showcases the skill, planning, and cooperation behind one of history's most effective hunting techniques.
Canada is home to
Baffin Island

, the fifth-largest island in the world, located in the Arctic Archipelago. Additionally, Canada's lakes contain one-fifth of the world's fresh water, making the country a key player in the global water supply. From vast landscapes to abundant freshwater, Canada is truly a land of immense natural wonders!

Election Day

! In Canada's 2025 federal election, the House of Commons expanded to 343 seats, reflecting the country's growing population. Each seat represents a unique riding, and the party that secures at least 172 seats forms a majority government. This adjustment emphasizes the importance of every vote, as even a single seat can influence the balance of power in Parliament.

Today is International Workers' Day, a day to recognize and celebrate the contributions of workers everywhere. It's also the anniversary of the passing of Gordon Lightfoot, the legendary Canadian singer-songwriter who famously wrote "The Canadian Railroad Trilogy." This song honours the navvies — the hardworking railway labourers who built Canada's transcontinental railway. The term navvy, derived from "navigator," referred to these workers who faced grueling, often perilous conditions as they laid tracks across the rugged Canadian landscape. Their efforts helped unite a vast country and were key to its expansion. While Canada celebrates Labour Day in September, today we remember the sacrifices of workers like the navvies, whose contributions continue to shape our nation.

The all-denim look, affectionately known as the Canadian tuxedo, has become a classic style statement! It all started in 1951 when American singer Bing Crosby was denied entry to a Vancouver hotel for wearing head-to-toe denim. Levi's heard about it and made him a custom denim tuxedo jacket to go with his denim jeans - and the rest is fashion history! Whether you wear your jeans dressed up or dressed down, they'll always have a place in Canadian style lore.

Latkes are crispy and golden potato pancakes that are a cherished part of Jewish cuisine and culture. While they’re famously associated with Hanukkah, these delicious treats are enjoyed year-round in Jewish households and beyond. This beloved dish is especially meaningful during
Canadian Jewish Heritage Month

, celebrated every May since 2018. Canada's Jewish population, the fourth largest in the world, has made incredible contributions to our country in areas like arts, science, and social justice. Celebrating Jewish heritage – and enjoying cultural dishes like latkes – is a delicious way to honour the diversity and traditions that enrich Canada.

Sumac isn't just a vibrant splash of red in a Canadian forest - it's a plant with a long history of cultural significance! Indigenous Peoples across Turtle Island have used sumac for generations, brewing its berries into refreshing teas and using its leaves in traditional medicines. While some varieties of sumac are used as a tart spice in Middle Eastern cuisine, here in Canada, the staghorn sumac is best known for its fuzzy red clusters and brilliant autumn colour. A true native plant with deep roots in our land's history!

Today is
Red Dress Day

in Canada — a day to honour and remember Missing and Murdered Indigenous Women, Girls, and Two-Spirit People (MMIWG2S). The red dresses hanging in windows and trees across the country serve as powerful symbols of those whose lives have been taken and those still missing. Traditional Indigenous clothing is often woven with deep cultural meaning, with intricate designs and patterns that reflect stories, spiritual beliefs, and connections to the land. These garments are more than just clothing — they are a form of storytelling, passed down through generations. On Red Dress Day, the red dresses themselves are a modern reflection of these woven traditions, symbolizing strength, resilience, and the ongoing call for justice.

Some species, like the Western Grebe, even perform synchronized "rushing" displays, where they dash across the water's surface together in a spectacular show of coordination!

It's tulip time in Ottawa! Every May, over a million tulip petals pop open across the capital for the
Canadian Tulip Festival

- a blooming beautiful tradition that started with a royal thank-you.  Back in 1945, the Dutch royal family sent 100,000 tulip bulbs to Canada to show their gratitude for sheltering Princess Juliana and her daughters during World War II. To ensure Princess Margriet was born a Dutch citizen, the maternity ward at the Ottawa Civic Hospital was temporarily declared international territory. That act of kindness planted the seeds of a friendship that still blossoms today - one tulip petal at a time!

From coast to coast, Canada is home to some wonderfully strange and amusing bylaws that reflect the quirks and character of its communities. In Dawson City, Yukon, it's illegal to disturb a moose, including honking at one! In Calgary, Alberta, you're not allowed to put your feet on benches, planters, or sculptures in public spaces. And In Souris, Prince Edward Island, you can't build a snowman taller than 30 inches on a corner lot—safety first, even for frosty friends! These odd rules show how unique (and Canadian!) our local bylaws can be.

Found in Southern Ontario, south of the Canadian Shield, the Eastern hog-nosed snake is a master of disguise and one of Canada's most intriguing reptiles.

Did you know that Jell-O has a sweet connection to Canada? In the 1930s and '40s, the iconic wobbly dessert became a pantry staple across the country thanks to clever advertising on Canadian radio and in magazines. Starting in 1906, jello was produced in Fort Erie, Ontario, and later in Cobourg, helping supply sweet, jiggly treats to Canadians coast to coast. From strawberry to lime, those colourful cubes became a fixture at church potlucks, school lunches, and yes, even hospital trays. Whether you slurped it, sliced it, or layered it in a retro salad, Jell-O has long had a place in Canadian kitchens!

June 6 marks the
81st anniversary of D-Day and the Battle of Normandy

. On D-Day—June 6, 1944—Canada showed the world what it means to be loyal. Over 14,000 Canadian troops landed on Juno Beach, fighting with unwavering dedication alongside Allied forces. Their loyalty to freedom, to each other, and to the mission remained steadfast even under relentless fire—a testament to the loyal spirit that defined Canada's role in the war and its lasting legacy in the fight for peace.

To commemorate the 81st anniversary of D-Day, please consider making a
donation to the Juno Beach Centre
to help them preserve this legacy for future generations through education and remembrance.
Nestled on Vancouver Island, the
Kinsol Trestle Bridge

is a stunning example of Canadian engineering and natural beauty. Built in 1920, this towering wooden bridge uses a Howe truss design—an ingenious system of beams that supports its massive length and height. At 44 metres tall and 187 metres long, the Kinsol Trestle is one of the tallest wooden trestle bridges in the world and a lasting symbol of Canada's timber heritage.

Whether it’s the cold winters that call for a warm, comforting bowl or the multicultural nature of the country, Canadians have welcomed ramen as part of their rich food scene!

The vison, or mink, is a small, agile mammal found throughout Canada’s forests and wetlands. Known for its sleek, glossy fur and impressive swimming skills, the vison hunts quietly along rivers and lakes. This clever predator helps keep ecosystems balanced and has long been valued by Indigenous peoples for its role in traditional hunting and trapping.

Canada’s vast landscape covers over 2 billion acres, with millions dedicated to fertile farmland. While Canada officially uses the metric system, the acre remains a popular way to measure land, especially in farming and real estate. These acres grow much of the food enjoyed across the country and around the world—from wheat and canola to fruits and vegetables. Canada’s farmland is a vital part of the nation’s economy, culture, and connection to the land.

In many Indigenous cultures across Canada, dreams are seen as powerful sources of guidance and healing. The dreamcatcher, which originated with the Anishinaabe people, is a handmade web traditionally hung above a bed to catch bad dreams while allowing good ones to pass through. Crafted from natural materials like willow and sinew, each part holds deep meaning—symbolizing protection, connection, and the cycle of life. Today, dreamcatchers remain a meaningful symbol of Indigenous identity and the enduring strength of traditions across the land.

The shako is a tall, cylindrical military cap once commonly worn by rifle regiments, including the
Queen’s Own Rifles of Canada

. In the 19th century, it was part of the formal uniform for officers and soldiers alike. The shako often features a cap badge displaying a crowned bugle horn—a symbol of light infantry and rifle regiments—along with the regiment’s name, reflecting a proud tradition of bravery and service.

Practised by many Indigenous nations along the Northwest Coast of Canada, the potlatch is a ceremonial gathering rooted in generosity, storytelling, music, and the sharing of wealth. Traditionally held to mark major life events like births, weddings, name-giving, and mourning, potlatches are also occasions to reaffirm community ties, pass down ancestral knowledge, and honour cultural laws. Though banned by the Canadian government from 1884 to 1951, potlatches endured and were carried forward with strength and spirit. Today, they continue as powerful expressions of identity, resilience, and cultural pride.

In Nova Scotia, Natal Day is celebrated on the first Monday in August and marks the "birth" of the Halifax–Dartmouth area. The name comes from the Latin natalis, meaning birth—fitting for a holiday that honours the founding of Halifax in 1749 and the growth of neighbouring communities. First celebrated in 1895, Natal Day has grown into a beloved long weekend tradition filled with parades, fireworks, concerts, and one of Canada’s oldest road races. It’s a joyful celebration of Nova Scotia’s history and coastal spirit!

The word inver comes from Scottish Gaelic, meaning “mouth of a river” or “river’s edge.” Many Canadian places with Scottish heritage use inver in their names—like Invermere, British Columbia and Inverness, Nova Scotia—to mark locations where rivers meet lakes or oceans. With over 8,500 rivers in Canada, these invers highlight the country’s strong connection to its waterways and natural landscapes.

A glyph is a carved or inscribed symbol—often holding cultural or linguistic meaning. In Canada, glyphs can be found etched into stone across the land, including ancient petroglyphs created by Indigenous peoples thousands of years ago. Ontario’s
Petroglyphs Provincial Park

is home to the largest known concentration of Indigenous rock carvings in Canada, with over 900 glyphs depicting animals, spirits, and sacred teachings. On the Prairies, Saskatchewan’s St. Victor Petroglyphs showcase more than 300 symbols carved into sandstone atop a coulee, believed to represent vision quests, hunting practices, and ceremonial life. These carvings are a powerful reminder of the deep connection between land, language, and story.

In Canada’s early one-room schoolhouses, students didn’t have notebooks or tablets—they used slates! These small, handheld chalkboards were a reusable and affordable way for kids to practise writing and arithmetic. With a slate pencil in hand, students could solve math problems, copy sentences, or spell out their lessons, then simply wipe it clean and start again. Slates were especially common in rural schools, where supplies were scarce and learning had to be practical. It’s a little piece of classroom history that helped shape generations of Canadian students!

Canada is broad in every sense of the word — stretching over 5,000 km from coast to coast, it’s the second-largest country in the world by land area! That vastness is beautifully captured in the second verse of O Canada, which includes the line: “How dear to us thy broad domain.” While the full version of the anthem isn’t often sung, these words remind us just how deeply Canadians cherish the sweeping landscapes, from rugged coastlines to prairie skies and northern lights.

Canada is home to some of the world’s most stealthy prowlers! Majestic creatures like cougars, wolves, and lynx can be spotted in forests and mountains across the country, often moving silently as they hunt for food. The elusive cougar, for example, roams areas from British Columbia to the Maritimes, mostly at dusk and dawn when they're hardest to spot. Next time you're hiking through Canadian wilderness, remember you may not see them - but some of Canada’s wild hunters could be on the prowl!

Happy
National Acadian Day

! On August 15, we celebrate the culture, history, and resilience of Acadian communities across Canada. After the Deportation of the Acadians in the 1700s, many were exiled from their Maritime homeland. Some eventually settled in Louisiana, where the word “Acadian” evolved into “Cajun.” While Cajun and Acadian cultures have distinct histories, Cajun traditions remain a vibrant reminder of Canada’s Acadian roots—rich in music, food, language, and pride that still echoes from the Maritimes to the bayou.

The word HOMES is a handy mnemonic many Canadians use to remember the names of the Great Lakes: Huron, Ontario, Michigan, Erie, and Superior. Together, these five vast freshwater lakes hold about 21% of the world’s surface fresh water and border both Canada and the United States. From boating and fishing to providing drinking water, the Great Lakes are an essential part of life and nature in Canada.

The ochre sea star is a tide pool icon found along the Pacific coast of Canada, especially in British Columbia. Ranging in colour from orange to purple, these five-armed wonders are keystone species—meaning they play a crucial role in maintaining the balance of marine ecosystems. Without them, mussel populations would take over! Some ochre sea stars can grow up to 50 cm across and live more than 20 years!

In Canada’s history, the cattle drive was an essential part of the ranching culture, especially in the western provinces. The tradition of cattle driving is still celebrated today in rodeos and ranching festivals, reminding Canadians of the tough, adventurous spirit that shaped the nation’s prairies.

The Bay of Fundy, located between New Brunswick and Nova Scotia, is home to the highest tides in the world—reaching up to 16 metres! This natural wonder fills and empties twice daily, moving over 160 billion tonnes of seawater. It’s also a hotspot for fossil discoveries, coastal hikes, and whale watching. Whether you're exploring Hopewell Rocks or catching a tidal bore, the Bay of Fundy is pure Canadian magic!

In the Okanagan Valley, renowned for its orchards and vineyards, grafting is a vital horticultural technique. Scion grafting involves joining a young shoot (the scion) from a prized fruit tree onto the rootstock of another, combining strengths to grow healthier, more productive plants. This method helps Okanagan growers cultivate delicious apples, cherries, and grapes suited to the region’s unique climate, supporting one of Canada’s most important fruit-growing areas.

An idiom is a phrase whose meaning isn’t obvious from the words themselves. Canada has plenty of its own: you might hear someone say “Give’r” to mean “go for it,” “double-double” for a coffee with two creams and two sugars, “snowbirds” for Canadians who head south in the winter, or the classic “Eh?” at the end of a sentence to check for agreement. These colourful expressions show how Canadians have turned everyday language into something uniquely their own, full of humour, culture, and local flavour.

A creel is a traditional woven basket once used by anglers to carry fish, keeping the catch fresh while navigating rivers and lakes. In Canada, creels were common from the trout streams of Ontario to the salmon rivers of British Columbia, often made from willow or split ash with careful craftsmanship. While no longer widely used, they’re still cherished today by fly fishers, heritage enthusiasts, and museums as a nod to Canada’s classic fishing traditions.

A genoa is a large foresail that overlaps the mainsail, designed to boost a sailboat’s speed, especially in lighter winds. It’s a common sight in Canadian sailing, from keelboat racing on Lake Ontario and the St. Lawrence to training fleets on the coastal waters of British Columbia. By maximizing wind power, the genoa helps sailors make the most of Canada’s diverse conditions on inland lakes and open seas alike.

From the Klondike Gold Rush to county fairs, Canada has a long history with “shills”—planted players or buyers who faked excitement to draw in a crowd. Gambling halls used shills to lure prospectors into card games, while travelling medicine shows had them pose as satisfied customers. Even in the 19th century, Canadian fur traders, lumber merchants, and land agents were sometimes accused of “shill bidding” at auctions—planting false bidders to raise prices.

Algae thrive in Canada’s waters, from the Atlantic coast to the Great Lakes and all the way to the Pacific. Green Cladophora in the Great Lakes supports fish and helps monitor water quality, while coastal seaweeds like rockweed shelter marine life along British Columbia’s shores. These simple organisms are unsung heroes, keeping Canada’s freshwater and marine ecosystems healthy.

Carts were essential for travel and trade in early Canada, and the
Red River Cart

is one of the most famous. Developed by the Métis during the settlement of the Prairies in the mid- to late-1800s, these two-wheeled wooden carts could carry up to 450 kg of goods across long distances, pulled by oxen or horses. The cart takes its name from the Red River, along which the Red River Colony was settled, and the Métis had their own Michif words for it, including aen wagon and aen charet. These carts became a lasting symbol of Métis ingenuity and resilience on the frontier.

Did you know one of Canada’s most iconic school supplies began with discarded cod skins? Sweat lodges are sacred spaces used by many First Nations, Métis, and Inuit communities for ceremony, healing, and spiritual renewal. Inside the lodge, steam rises as water is poured over heated stones, creating an intense sweat that symbolizes purification and connection to the land, ancestors, and community. In Enderby, British Columbia, you’ll find the world’s largest non-motorized lawnmower—an enormous 7-metre tall replica of a classic reel mower. Built by Herb Higginbottom between 2007 and 2009, it’s an exact scale-up of the smaller mower displayed beside it. Today, the giant mower stands proudly on a patch of astroturf next to Herb’s Deep Creek Tool Museum, celebrating both ingenuity and a bit of Canadian lawn-loving humour.

Canada is home to several unique dog breeds that reflect its history and climate. The Newfoundland, strong and skilled in swimming, helped fishermen in coastal waters, while the Labrador Retriever, originally from Newfoundland, was bred to assist fishermen by retrieving nets and fish. These Canadian breeds showcase skill, resilience, and a special bond with people across the country.

Today is
World Polio Day

! Canadian researchers at Toronto’s Connaught Laboratories played a key role in developing the polio vaccine. In the late 1940s, Raymond Parker, Joseph Morgan, and Helen Morton created Medium 199, the world’s first synthetic nutrient formula for growing cells. In 1951, Arthur Franklin used Medium 199 to grow the poliovirus, giving Dr. Jonas Salk the tools to develop the first polio vaccine. Their work helped make safe testing possible and led to polio’s eradication in Canada by the 1970s. By 1994, the World Health Organization declared the Americas polio-free, a milestone made possible by decades of vaccination efforts and Canadian ingenuity.

Today is International Inuit Day! The Thule were the ancestors of today’s Inuit, emerging around 1000 CE and migrating eastward from Alaska across the Arctic. They settled in what is now northern Canada and Greenland, adapting brilliantly to one of the world’s harshest environments. Expert hunters and innovators, the Thule developed tools such as dog sleds, harpoons, and umiaks (large skin boats). Their ingenuity and resilience laid the foundation for Inuit cultures that continue to thrive across Canada’s North today.

Measured in hertz, radio waves power the sounds and signals that surround us – and Canadian inventor Reginald Fessenden helped make it all possible. Born in Quebec, Fessenden was a true pioneer of wireless communication. In 1906, he made history by broadcasting the first voice and music over radio waves, sending “O Holy Night” across the air. His groundbreaking work paved the way for radio, television, and the technology that connects us today. He also laid the groundwork for AM radio, sonar, and even early radar – innovations that still echo through modern communication.

Caribou stew is a traditional Canadian stew and a specialty of Nunavut cuisine. Today, caribou stew remains a cherished part of Canadian culinary heritage, celebrated for its rich flavours and cultural significance.

Made by adding kefir grains to milk, it produces a tangy, slightly effervescent beverage packed with beneficial bacteria. Across Canada, kefir is enjoyed in smoothies, breakfasts, and snacks, reflecting Canadians’ growing interest in gut health and nutritious, fermented foods.

Enoki mushrooms, with their long, slender stems and tiny white caps, are a popular ingredient in Canadian kitchens, especially in soups, stir-fries, and hot pots. While they’re native to East Asia, enokis are now grown in Canada year-round, valued for their crisp texture and mild, slightly fruity flavour. Their popularity reflects Canada’s diverse food culture, where global flavours are celebrated and enjoyed across the country!

The coypu, known in Canada as the nutria, is a large, semi-aquatic rodent originally from South America that was introduced here for the fur trade. While it might look a bit like a beaver or muskrat, it has a distinctive long, round, sparsely-haired tail. Coypu were once raised on fur farms in provinces like British Columbia, Alberta, and Ontario, making them an interesting, if little-known, part of Canada’s fur-trading history.

In Canada, thrums are the soft wool bits knit inside thrummed mittens to keep hands extra warm in the coldest winters. This cozy technique, believed to have originated from Indigenous and Newfoundland knitting traditions, creates a fluffy lining that traps heat—perfect for those chilly Canadian days! Whether you’re outside skating or shoveling snow, thrummed mittens are a true winter essential.

The doner kebab, a Turkish dish of spiced meat cooked on a rotating spit, inspired a Canadian classic - the Halifax donair! Adapted in Nova Scotia in the 1970s, it swaps lamb for seasoned ground beef and yogurt sauce for a sweet garlic sauce. Served on warm pita with diced onions and tomatoes, the donair quickly became a Maritime favourite and a late-night legend. The donair became so iconic it was named Halifax’s official food in 2015.

Before Canada adopted the metric system in the 1970s, distances were measured in miles - a reminder of which still lingers in some old road signs and stories! Once a major hazard for Klondike Gold Rush stampeders navigating the Yukon River, this dramatic canyon features striking basalt cliffs formed by ancient lava flows and stunning emerald-green waters rushing beneath.

From beloved classics like Lucy Maud Montgomery's Anne of Green Gables to influential contemporary novels such as Margaret Atwood’s The Handmaid’s Tale, the Canadian literary canon also includes powerful works by Indigenous authors whose stories reflect the depth and diversity of this land. Together, these books remain cornerstones of Canadian storytelling.

Across parts of Canada with deep Scottish roots, you might spot a curious artifact known as a snuff mull. Today, snuff mulls are cherished as cultural keepsakes, reflecting the Scottish heritage woven into Canadian communities from Nova Scotia to the Prairies.

Peary caribou were listed as endangered under the Species at Risk Act in 2011.

Ferguson “Fergie” Jenkins, one of Canada’s greatest baseball pitchers, made history as the first Canadian to win the Cy Young Award. He retired with 284 wins, and was the first pitcher in MLB history to record 3,000+ strikeouts while allowing fewer than 1,000 walks. Inducted into the

Did you know that in Canada, even your change has a limit? Under the
Currency Act

, there are caps on how much you can use in coins for one purchase: up to $25 in loonies, $40 in toonies, $10 in dimes, $10 in quarters, and just $5 in nickels. These limits exist to stop “coin overload” at the counter – so while a store can accept more, they don’t have to once those limits are reached. So next time, leave the big bag of loonies and toonies at home!

Lakers are massive freighters built to carry bulk Canadian cargo, including grain, iron ore, salt, and limestone. Since the late 19th century, they have hauled raw materials from docks in the Great Lakes and St. Lawrence Seaway to the industrial centres of Ontario and Quebec. Many lakers are specially designed with narrow beams and long hulls to fit the waterways’ locks and channels, making them unique to Canada’s inland shipping routes.

Inlay artwork is a decorative technique that embeds one material within another to create patterns or designs. Indigenous artists in Canada have long used inlay to add meaning and beauty to their work, incorporating materials like shell, bone, stone, or metal into wood and other surfaces. The technique appears in carvings, tools, ceremonial objects, and jewelry, with patterns that often reflect stories, symbols, and connections to the land.

Gem-quality varieties like aquamarine and occasional emeralds have been discovered in Ontario, the Northwest Territories, and Newfoundland, making Canada a hidden gem for mineral collectors. These sparkling crystals form in granite and pegmatite rocks and dazzle with their brilliant colour.

Across Canada, many beloved businesses are family owned and operated, with traditions and know-how passed down through generations. From corner stores and hardware shops to local cafés and diners, these family-run enterprises are deeply rooted in the communities they serve. Built on trust, pride, and a personal touch, they continue to play a vital role in Canada’s economy and daily life.

A bight is a wide, curved section of coastline where the land bends inward, creating calm, sheltered waters. In Canada, Trinity Bight in Newfoundland and Labrador is a classic example, with shores that have supported fishing, transport, and local communities for centuries. These natural curves help shape ecosystems and provide safe harbours along the rugged Atlantic shoreline.

Built for speed, pronghorns are the fastest land mammals in Canada, capable of reaching speeds of up to 90 km/h across the open prairies of southern Alberta and Saskatchewan. Their signature “prongs” are especially rare in the animal kingdom – true horns that shed and regrow each year, unlike the permanent horns of most animals.

The Girl Who Cried Pearls

made history in 2026 by winning the Academy Award for Best Animated Short Film. Produced by the National Film Board of Canada, the stop-motion film was created by Montréal-based directors Chris Lavis and Maciek Szczerbowski, and is set in early 20th-century Montréal. Featuring Canadian voice actor Colm Feore, it’s a powerful example of homegrown storytelling earning global recognition.

Long before modern handbags, Indigenous artisans across Canada crafted beautiful and functional purses using materials from the land. Caribou hide was carefully prepared for durability and softness, then adorned with intricate glass beadwork introduced through early trade. These handmade purses were not only practical – they often carried deep cultural meaning, with patterns and colours reflecting identity, community, and tradition.

Canada’s early auto industry relied on strong, durable axles to keep vehicles rolling across rugged roads. In the 1920s and 1930s, factories in Ontario and Quebec built cars and trucks with heavy-duty axles that could handle both city streets and rural trails. These simple yet vital components were essential to the growth of Canada’s automotive industry and its expanding national transportation network.

Being silly is a proud Canadian tradition. Canadians invented the modern Whoopee Cushion in Toronto in the 1930s, and since then, April Fools’ Day has been celebrated with funny news stories, cheeky tricks, and harmless pranks across the country. From silly shenanigans to playful jokes among friends, Canada’s tradition of silliness keeps the laughs rolling every April 1.

The Ruddy Duck is one of Canada’s smallest diving ducks, with a compact chestnut body, sky-blue bill, and stiff, upright tail. About 86% of its breeding population nests in wetlands and reservoirs of the prairie pothole region. Males are known for lively courtship displays, sometimes drumming the water with their bills to impress a mate!

A chock is a wedge used to keep wheels or sleds from rolling, and in Canada’s snowy north, chocks are essential for sleds and dog teams. Chocks can be made of wood, rubber, metal, plastic, or even ice, helping adventurers and mushers stay safe on slippery trails. This simple tool proves that even the smallest pieces can have a big impact in the great outdoors!

A logan is a boulder balanced so delicately it can be rocked with ease, but Canada’s Mount Logan is anything but. Rising 5,959 metres in Yukon’s Kluane National Park, it’s the country’s highest peak, with a base circumference even larger than that of Mount Everest. This “logan” is one that definitely won’t be budged!

Happy Easter from Canuckle! Canada’s tiniest sandpipers are affectionately called “peeps,” just like the fluffy chicks we associate with Easter. The Least Sandpiper flits through wetlands each spring in small, energetic flocks. These tiny birds have bright yellow legs and can weigh less than a golf ball, making them one of North America’s lightest shorebirds.

In the House of Commons, MPs with leadership roles sit on the front bench, while everyone else sits on the back bench. Backbenchers may not wear the cabinet title, but they’re far from idle, reviewing legislation in committees, introducing private members’ bills, and taking part in Question Period. In the 45th Canadian Parliament, all 343 MPs, including the backbench, keep the work of democracy moving every day.

About one in ten Canadians is left-handed. One famous lefty is Canadian golfer Mike Weir, who made history in 2003 by winning The Masters Tournament and becoming the first Canadian to capture golf’s iconic green jacket. Weir is Canada’s most celebrated left-handed golfer, and every Masters week fans remember his historic victory on the sport’s biggest stage.
